unit.note: client vitest 206 files / 2016 passed (main: 205 / 2007). Server suite re-run green and unaffected (176 files / 2046 passed + 1 skipped, SHIPWRIGHT_NETWORK_PROFILE=local). New test files: useTerminalResize.atlas-heal.test.ts (14 hook tests), EmbeddedTerminal.atlas-heal.test.tsx (4 component tests joining the wiring seam). Extended: activation-repaint.test.ts (+7), xtermAddons.atlas.test.ts (+8, incl. 3 GPU-context-loss), useTerminalResize.test-harness.ts (atlasHeal seam). Production: webgl-atlas-repaint.ts exposes its coalesced heal (pending now reset in finally); xtermAddons.ts surfaces handle.healAtlas (WebGL arm only, assigned after loadAddon succeeds, retracted on context loss); activation-repaint.ts fires it on every trailing pass; useTerminalResize.ts gates it on active (read at fire time); EmbeddedTerminal.tsx holds the ref. EmbeddedTerminal.tsx held at its bloat baseline (exactly 314; anti_ratchet_check.py --worktree exit 0). tsc + oxlint clean; npm run build green.

integration.note: EmbeddedTerminal.atlas-heal.test.tsx mounts the REAL component over mocked @xterm/* doubles and drives the real hook chain (useTerminalResize -> activation-repaint -> the webgl-atlas-repaint fence -> term.clearTextureAtlas). Added because the two unit suites each covered one half of the seam and would BOTH have stayed green if the wiring were deleted (code-review MED). The trailing passes are advanced as separate macrotasks with a microtask drain between them, so the fence coalescing is modelled the way the browser does it -- a single-tick advance collapsed the two heals into one and passed for the wrong reason.

e2e.note: client/e2e/flows/94-webgl-atlas-repaint.spec.ts, real Chromium against an isolated single-process stack (built client + SHIPWRIGHT_STATIC_DIR + tsx server on :3947, temp USERPROFILE). Pre-existing test still green (renderer=webgl, canvases=3, atlasRepaints=5). NEW re-show test: baseline=1, immediate=1 (the heal is NOT synchronous on the event -- pins the plan-review correction), after=3 (two clears per re-show: the 130 ms + 350 ms passes), settled count unchanged (no feedback loop). Probe: window.__embeddedTerminalAtlasRepaints.

surface_verification.justification: Client-only rendering fix (WebGL glyph atlas). Executed in a real browser against an isolated stack on :3947 -- the deployed :3847 serves an older client build. The WebGL renderer cannot be exercised in jsdom, so a real-browser run is the only proof the heal reaches the LIVE addon.

test_completeness.enumeration_basis: The 5 acceptance criteria in .shipwright/planning/iterate/2026-07-14-terminal-atlas-heal-on-refocus.md, plus every branch introduced by the four review rounds (external plan review, code review, external code review, doubt review) = 14 behaviors.

behaviors.behavior: AC-1a: a window focus / visibilitychange / pageshow (bfcache) restore drives the atlas heal
AC-1b: the in-app Transcript->Terminal tab activation drives the atlas heal
AC-2 (timing): the heal is never synchronous on the event and rides EVERY trailing pass (two shots)
AC-2 (coalescing): an event burst costs ONE set of passes, not one set per event
AC-3: no heal while the document is hidden or the Terminal tab is inactive (the canvas never composites)
The active gate is read at FIRE time, not at schedule time
AC-3: a disposed / unmounted terminal is never cleared (a queued heal is cancelled)
AC-3: the DOM-renderer arm exposes no heal handle; the refresh passes still run
WebGL activation throws (GPU-blacklisted host) -> no heal handle
A GPU context loss retracts the heal AND cancels a heal already queued on the fence
AC-4: the #206 fences hold -- onAddTextureAtlasCanvas unsubscribed, heal deferred, never wired to the data / scroll / resize paths
A throwing heal does not break the trailing pass (mid-dispose term)
AC-5: the heal reaches the LIVE WebGL renderer on a real GPU and does not feedback-loop
behaviors.evidence: e2e flow spec 94, executed (renderer=webgl, canvases=3, re-show delta=2, settled count unchanged)

behaviors.behavior: The wrong letters are VISUALLY gone on the user GPU
behaviors.evidence: SwiftShader (headless Chromium) neither evicts nor repacks GPU textures, so it cannot reproduce GPU-specific glyph corruption -- the same residual #206 and #167 carried. Resolved by user UAT after the deploy.
